content:
# Judges Chapter 8 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_276.jpg, JDG_page_277.jpg

1. Then the men of Ephraim said to him, "What is this you have done to us, not to call us when you went to fight against Midian?" And they accused him fiercely.

2. And he said to them, "What have I done now in comparison with you? Is not the gleaning of the grapes of Ephraim better than the grape harvest of Abiezer?

3. God has given into your hands the princes of Midian, Oreb and Zeeb. What have I been able to do in comparison with you?" Then their anger against him subsided when he said this.

4. And Gideon came to the Jordan and crossed over, he and the 300 men who were with him, exhausted yet pursuing.

5. And he said to the men of Succoth, "Please give loaves of bread to the people who follow me, for they are exhausted, and I am pursuing after Zebah and Zalmunna, the kings of Midian."

6. And the officials of Succoth said, "Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna already in your hand, that we should give bread to your army?"

7. So Gideon said, "Well then, when the LORD has given Zebah and Zalmunna into my hand, I will flail your flesh with the thorns of the wilderness and with briers."

8. And from there he went up to Penuel and spoke to them in the same way, and the men of Penuel answered him as the men of Succoth had answered.

9. And he said to the men of Penuel, "When I come again in peace, I will break down this tower."

10. Now Zebah and Zalmunna were in Karkor with their army, about 15,000 men, all who were left of all the army of the people of the east, for there had fallen 120,000 men who drew the sword.

11. And Gideon went up by the way of the tent dwellers east of Nobah and Jogbehah and attacked the army, for the army felt secure.

12. And Zebah and Zalmunna fled, and he pursued them and captured the two kings of Midian, Zebah and Zalmunna, and he threw all the army into a panic.

13. Then Gideon the son of Joash returned from the battle by the ascent of Heres.

14. And he captured a young man of Succoth and questioned him. And he wrote down for him the officials and elders of Succoth, seventy-seven men.

15. And he came to the men of Succoth and said, "Behold Zebah and Zalmunna, about whom you taunted me, saying, 'Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna already in your hand, that we should give bread to your men who are exhausted?'"

16. And he took the elders of the city, and he took thorns of the wilderness and briers, and with them he taught the men of Succoth a lesson.

17. And he broke down the tower of Penuel and killed the men of the city.

18. Then he said to Zebah and Zalmunna, "Where are the men whom you killed at Tabor?" They answered, "As you are, so were they. Every one of them resembled the son of a king."

19. And he said, "They were my brothers, the sons of my mother. As the LORD lives, if you had saved them alive, I would not kill you."

20. So he said to Jether his firstborn, "Rise and kill them!" But the young man did not draw his sword, for he was afraid, because he was still a young man.

21. Then Zebah and Zalmunna said, "Rise yourself and fall upon us, for as the man is, so is his strength." And Gideon arose and killed Zebah and Zalmunna, and he took the crescent ornaments that were on the necks of their camels.

22. Then the men of Israel said to Gideon, "Rule over us, you and your son and your grandson also, for you have saved us from the hand of Midian."

23. Gideon said to them, "I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you. The LORD will rule over you."

24. And Gideon said to them, "Let me make a request of you: every one of you give me the earrings from his spoil." (For they had golden earrings, because they were Ishmaelites.)

25. And they answered, "We will willingly give them." And they spread a garment, and every man threw in it the earrings of his spoil.

26. And the weight of the golden earrings that he requested was 1,700 shekels of gold, besides the crescent ornaments and the pendants and the purple garments worn by the kings of Midian, and besides the collars that were around the necks of their camels.

27. And Gideon made an ephod of it and put it in his city, in Ophrah. And all Israel whored after it there, and it became a snare to Gideon and to his family.

28. So Midian was subdued before the people of Israel, and they raised their heads no more. And the land had rest forty years in the days of Gideon.

29. Jerubbaal the son of Joash went and lived in his own house.

30. Now Gideon had seventy sons, his own offspring, for he had many wives.

31. And his concubine who was in Shechem also bore him a son, and he called his name Abimelech.

32. And Gideon the son of Joash died in a good old age and was buried in the tomb of Joash his father, at Ophrah of the Abiezrites.

33. As soon as Gideon died, the people of Israel turned again and whored after the Baals and made Baal-berith their god.

34. And the people of Israel did not remember the LORD their God, who had delivered them from the hand of all their enemies on every side,

35. and they did not show steadfast love to the family of Jerubbaal (that is, Gideon) in return for all the good that he had done to Israel.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 8 completes the Gideon cycle — and the descent is swift. The man who refused kingship (v.23) takes the spoils of a king (v.26), creates a priestly object that becomes an idol (v.27), accumulates wives and sons like a monarch (v.30), and names a son from his concubine "My Father Is King" — Abimelech (v.31). The chapter sets up the horror of chapter 9.

**Verse 4 — "exhausted yet pursuing":** ayephim v'rodphim — one of the most economical and powerful descriptions of soldiers in the Hebrew Bible. Two words that capture the entire paradox of Gideon's campaign: human weakness and divine purpose.

**Verse 16 — "taught":** The Hebrew vayyoda (from yada', "to know") is a brutal euphemism. Gideon "made known" to the men of Succoth by flailing them with thorns. The gap between the mild verb and the violent action is the narrator's comment.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.16 — "taught the men of Succoth a lesson" vs. "punished them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he taught the men of Sukkot" | vayyoda bahem — literal |
| LOGOS | "he taught the men of Succoth a lesson" | Same with slight expansion |
| PNEUMA | "punished them with thorns and briers" | Interprets vayyoda as punishment |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew vayyoda bahem (hiphil of yada', "to make known") is a deliberate euphemism — the narrator uses a mild verb ("taught/made known") for a violent act. PNEUMA's "punished" is accurate to the event but loses the ironic understatement. The text's use of "taught" for torture is part of its dark narrative style.

**Status:** Moderate. Literary euphemism — tone matters.

---

### v.26 — "1,700 shekels of gold" vs. "forty-three pounds of gold"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "one thousand seven hundred shekels of gold" | Exact Hebrew measure |
| LOGOS | "1,700 shekels of gold" | Same, numeral format |
| PNEUMA | "forty-three pounds of gold" | Converts to modern weight |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Preserving the ancient unit (shekels) maintains the text's historical context. A shekel is approximately 11.5 grams; 1,700 shekels is approximately 19.5 kg or 43 lbs. PNEUMA's conversion is approximately correct but introduces a modern measurement system foreign to the ancient world. Readers interested in the weight can consult a footnote or study Bible.

**Status:** Minor. Recurring ancient measures question.

---

### v.27 — "whored after it" vs. "prostituted themselves by worshiping it"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "went whoring after it" | vayyiznu acharav — literal |
| LOGOS | "whored after it" | Same, slightly more concise |
| PNEUMA | "prostituted themselves by worshiping it" | Adds "by worshiping" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew zanah acharei ("to whore after") is the standard Judges metaphor for idolatry — the same expression used in 2:17. PNEUMA adds "by worshiping it," which is explanatory but unnecessary — the prostitution metaphor already implies worship of a false object. More importantly, the addition domesticates the metaphor's shock value. The text intends the sexual metaphor to disturb.

**Status:** Minor. Consistent with ch.2 resolution on the same idiom.

---

### v.35 — "steadfast love" vs. "loyalty"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "loyalty" | chesed — covenant faithfulness |
| LOGOS | "steadfast love" | Standard rendering of chesed |
| PNEUMA | "loyalty" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. chesed is one of the richest words in the Hebrew Bible — it encompasses loyalty, faithfulness, lovingkindness, and covenant obligation. "Steadfast love" (the standard formal rendering) captures more of the word's range than "loyalty" alone, which reduces chesed to a single dimension. In this context, Israel owed Gideon's family more than mere loyalty — they owed them enduring, covenantal gratitude.

**Status:** Minor. Major Hebrew concept — minor translation variance.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA explains euphemism | 1 | "Taught" → "punished" (v.16) |
| PNEUMA converts ancient measures | 1 | Shekels → pounds (v.26) |
| PNEUMA adds explanatory words | 1 | "Whored after" → "prostituted themselves by worshiping" (v.27) |
| Lexical choice for chesed | 1 | "Steadfast love" vs. "loyalty" (v.35) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 8 continues the established pattern. PNEUMA's additions and explanations make the text more accessible but consistently reduce its literary power. The most significant choice is v.16, where the narrator's euphemistic "taught" — a word hiding violence — is flattened to the interpretive "punished."

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
